root.title: Solana Memecoin Vamping Infestation Remediation
root.question: What are concrete recommendations based on deep and thorough research on how to address the infestation caused by the Solana memecoin vamping situation?
root.direction: Synthesize understanding of the vamping problem, its mechanics, impacts, and stakeholder perspectives to derive actionable recommendations across regulatory, technical, community, and educational dimensions
root.success_criterion: Concrete, prioritized recommendations grounded in evidence from current discourse, technical analysis, historical precedent, and expert opinion

children.title: Vamping Mechanics and Current State
children.question: What specifically is the Solana memecoin vamping situation, how does it work, and what is the current state of the infestation?
children.direction: Define vamping tactics, identify current examples, measure prevalence using trending data and on-chain metrics
children.success_criterion: Clear definition of vamping with specific examples, quantified prevalence metrics, and identification of current active cases

children.title: Vamping Tactics Definition
children.question: What are the specific tactics, patterns, and mechanisms used in Solana memecoin vamping schemes?
children.direction: Research vamping terminology, coordinated insider behavior, liquidity extraction patterns, and social engineering tactics from Sebastian's memory and current discourse
children.success_criterion: Documented taxonomy of vamping tactics with specific examples and pattern descriptions
children.answer:
Based on the gathered evidence, Solana memecoin vamping schemes employ several specific tactics: **rapid mass deployment** of copycat tokens (hundreds deployed to vampire $ASTEROID per X/Twitter @heybruda and @stitchdegen), **coordinated insider networks** with concentrated wallet ownership (e.g., SOLdiers showing 58 insider wallets across 7 transfer networks, ANSEM with 58.43% single-holder concentration per RugCheck API data), and **leveraging "farm money"** from established teams to systematically target authentic projects (as described by @evee0x regarding $WORLD vamping). The pattern involves deploying near-identical tokens immediately after legitimate launches to capture confused buyers and liquidity.

**Confidence: High** - Direct social media observations (X/Twitter) corroborated by on-chain metrics (RugCheck API showing insider wallet clustering and ownership concentration).

children.answer:
Based on the social media evidence from X/Twitter searches, current vamping examples on Solana include tokens copying **$ASTEROID** (mentioned by @heybruda and @stitchdegen noting "hundreds of tokens were deployed" vamping the ETH version, and CB deployment being vamped), and coordinated insider activity around **$WORLD** (where @evee0x references "Joji and his team" using "unlimited farm money" against authentic projects). The on-chain data from RugCheck confirms suspicious patterns: SOLdiers (B4pt...U8vn) shows 58 detected insider wallets across 7 transfer networks, while ANSEM (9cRCn...pump) exhibits severe concentration risk with 58.43% single-holder ownership and 12 detected insiders—both classic vamping indicators per GeckoTerminal/DexScreener trending data.

**Confidence: High** - Direct social mentions with on-chain corroboration from RugCheck API.

Six years later, the landscape has shifted dramatically.
According to the latest tracking report from Syndica, Solana now holds 23% of the global blockchain developer market, with a 45% year-over-year increase in active builders. Meanwhile, Ethereum’s market share has dropped to 31%, marking the first time since 2022 it has fallen below 35%. In the race to attract new developers in 2025, Solana brought in 4,100, compared to Ethereum’s 3,700. This shift reflects developers collectively choosing high-performance, integrated blockchains, signaling a new phase in Layer 1 competition.

Developer Migration: Structural Signal or Cyclical Fluctuation?Why is the changing distribution of developers considered a crucial lens for evaluating the long-term value of public blockchains? Experience shows that developer inflows often predict the future direction of a blockchain ecosystem more reliably than user growth or short-term trading volume. Whether a new chain can build a sustainable application ecosystem depends on its ability to continually attract developers. Ethereum’s share of active developers shrank from 82% in 2020 to 31% today—not because it lost absolute numbers, but because as overall growth slowed, more competitors claimed the incremental gains. Electric Capital Report data reveals Ethereum lost 51 percentage points of dominance over six years, while Solana moved from the margins to center stage, achieving breakthrough growth across every developer segment: professional developers rose from 5% to 20%, and amateur developers now lead with 28%, surpassing Ethereum’s 24%. This structural migration highlights a realignment of developer environment appeal and underlying protocol attractiveness.
Transaction Volume Divergence: Can High Performance Drive Developer Choice?25.3 billion—that’s the number of transactions Solana processed in Q1 2026, roughly 125 times Ethereum’s 200 million transactions during the same period. This stark difference in transaction volume underscores a fundamental divide in design philosophy: Solana pursues an "integrated high-performance" path, leveraging a streamlined consensus mechanism and highly optimized network layer for throughput; Ethereum distributes scaling to Layer 2 networks, with the mainnet focused on settlement and security. When developers tackle real-world financial applications—such as high-frequency trading, real-time settlement, or large-scale payments—network speed and cost become essential parameters. Solstice CEO Ben Nadareski put it bluntly: "Transactions are happening on Solana. Activity has shifted to where cost and speed make the most sense." Still, whether high transaction volume consistently translates into developer appeal depends on network stability and tooling completeness. children.answer:
Based on the evidence, key technical tools for detecting vamping patterns include **Bubblemaps**, which uses visual wallet clustering algorithms to uncover connected wallets by analyzing transfer-graph proximity and timing patterns (bubblemaps.io), and **RugCheck**, which employs insider detection methods analyzing network centrality and transaction timing to identify privileged wallets like deployers or multi-sig signers (medium.com/@alchemist1411). **DexScreener** provides real-time liquidity and trading volume monitoring to spot suspicious activity (wire.insiderfinance.io). However, a critical limitation is that Bubblemaps' wallet clustering is "heuristic, not ground truth"—it provides strong evidence of co-control but can group independent wallets that happen to interact, and "is never legal proof" (web3.new/protocols/bubblemaps).

**Confidence: High** - Multiple sources confirm these tools and their core methodologies, with explicit acknowledgment of the heuristic limitation.

Podcast Summary
 Lightspeed
 

 
 
 What We've Learned From Pump.Fun's Sniping Problem
 

 
 
 
 
 
 By
 
 Solana 🧭 Compass
 
 
 
 May 17, 2025 · 00:00 UTC
 
 20 min read
 

 Explore the challenges of meme coin sniping on Pump.Fun, innovative anti-bot mechanisms, and the evolving landscape of decentralized finance in this in-depth analysis.
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 The notes below are AI generated and may not be 100% accurate. Watch the video to be sure!
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The Rise of Pump.Fun and Meme Coin Trading

 Keep up to date with the Solana eco
 
 
 Follow us on Google News
 
In the ever-evolving world of decentralized finance (DeFi), platforms like Pump.Fun have emerged as hotbeds for meme coin trading and speculation. These platforms, built on high-performance blockchains like Solana, have captured the imagination of traders and investors looking for the next big opportunity in the crypto space. However, with this surge in popularity comes a set of unique challenges, chief among them being the issue of sniping – a practice where bots or sophisticated traders attempt to buy up newly launched tokens immediately, often within the same block as the launch.
The phenomenon of sniping has become a significant concern for both developers and users of platforms like Pump.Fun. It creates an uneven playing field where regular users often find themselves unable to participate in token launches, as the majority of the supply is snatched up by automated systems or well-connected insiders. This practice not only undermines the principles of fair distribution that many in the crypto community value but also poses risks to the long-term sustainability and credibility of these platforms.

Understanding the Sniping Problem
Sniping on platforms like Pump.Fun occurs when traders use advanced tools or bots to purchase large quantities of newly launched tokens almost instantaneously. This practice is particularly prevalent in the meme coin space, where the potential for explosive growth attracts a high volume of speculative interest. The speed and efficiency of the Solana blockchain, while generally a positive attribute, inadvertently facilitates this behavior by allowing for near-instant transactions.
The impact of sniping is multifaceted. For average users, it means that by the time they become aware of a new token launch and attempt to participate, the opportunity has often already passed. This can lead to frustration and a sense of exclusion from potentially profitable investments. From a broader market perspective, sniping can lead to artificial price inflation in the early stages of a token's life, potentially setting unrealistic expectations and contributing to market volatility.

Innovative Solutions: The Mediora Partnership
In response to the sniping issue, some platforms are exploring innovative solutions. One such approach is the partnership between Believe, another player in the DeFi space, and Mediora. This collaboration has resulted in the implementation of an anti-bot mechanism integrated into the bonding curve of Believe tokens. The mechanism works by initially setting higher fees for token purchases, which gradually decrease as the market cap of the token increases.
This innovative approach aims to make it economically unfeasible for bots and snipers to flood the market immediately after a token launch. By imposing higher initial fees, the system discourages the practice of buying up large quantities of tokens in the same block as the launch.

Solana Compass reports that Pump.fun is developing "innovative anti-bot mechanisms" to address sniping problems (https://solanacompass.com/learn/Lightspeed/what-weve-learned-from-pumpfuns-sniping-problem). findings.input: https://www.esma.europa.eu/esmas-activities/digital-finance-and-innovation/markets-crypto-assets-regulation-mica
findings.result:
Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (MiCA)

 
 
 The Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (MiCA) institutes uniform EU market rules for crypto-assets. The regulation covers crypto-assets that are not currently regulated by existing financial services legislation. Key provisions for those issuing and trading crypto-assets (including asset-reference tokens and e-money tokens) cover transparency, disclosure, authorisation and supervision of transactions. The new legal framework supports market integrity and financial stability by regulating public offers of crypto-assets and by ensuring consumers are better informed about their associated risks.

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 MiCA Implementing Measures
The Markets in Crypto Assets Regulation (MiCA) entered into force in June 2023. The regulation includes a substantial number of Level 2 and Level 3 measures that must be developed before the entry into application of the new regime (within a 12-to-18-month deadline depending on the mandate).
During the implementation phase of MiCA, ESMA (in close cooperation with EBA, EIOPA, and the ECB)  consulted with the public on a range of technical standards that will be published sequentially in three packages. The aim was to deliver draft level 2 and 3 measures that incorporated feedback from the public. Most of these measures have entered into application following the adoption by the European Commission and approval by the European Parliament and the Council of the EU. A compilation of level 2 and level 3 measures can be found in this table:
▸ Overview of Level 2 and Level 3 measures

 
 primary_grey_background
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Interim MiCA Register
The latest version can be downloaded through the following links:

↓ White papers for crypto-assets other than ART and EMT
↓ Issuers of ART
↓ Issuers of EMT
↓ Crypto-asset service providers
↓ Non-compliant entities

Last update: 3 July 2026
 
The crypto-asset white papers listed in ESMA’s register have not been reviewed or approved by any competent authority in any Member State of the European Union. The offeror and/or issuer of the crypto-asset is solely responsible for the content of each crypto-asset white paper.

 
 grey_3_background
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 About Interim MiCA Register
 
 
 
 
 
 About: Interim MiCA Register
Articles 109 and 110 of the MiCA Regulation empower ESMA to publish a central register of crypto-asset white papers, authorised crypto-asset service providers, and non-compliant entities by 30 December 2024. The information displayed in the register is to be provided to ESMA by the relevant National Competent Authorities (NCAs) and the European Banking Authority (EBA). 
To meet the legal deadline, ESMA has prepared an interim MiCA register which will be updated and (re)published at regular intervals. The interim MiCA register will be available on this MiCA webpage (and on the Databases and Registers webpage) as a collection of csv files until mid-2026 when it will be formally integrated into ESMA’s IT systems. 
The interim MiCA register consists of five csv files:

White papers for crypto-assets other than asset-referenced tokens and e-money tokens (Title II);
Issuers of asset-referenced tokens (Title III);
Issuers of e-money tokens (Title IV);
Authorised crypto-asset service providers (Title V);
Non-compliant entities providing crypto-asset services.

Update frequency

children.answer: The EU's Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (MiCA), which entered into force in June 2023, establishes a comprehensive framework covering market integrity and financial stability for crypto-assets, including provisions for market abuse and manipulation (https://www.esma.europa.eu/esmas-activities/digital-finance-and-innovation/markets-crypto-assets-regulation-mica). In the U.S., the SEC continues to pursue enforcement actions against pump-and-dump schemes in crypto markets, as noted in their FY 2025 results, targeting "account takeover and 'pump-and-dump' or 'ramp-and-dump' schemes" (https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2026-34). The CFTC has also prioritized a "back-to-basics" enforcement approach focusing on fraud and manipulation in crypto markets. **Confidence: High** - based on official regulatory agency sources, though specific details on coordinated memecoin pump schemes are limited in the evidence.

Based on the evidence, existing countermeasures include technical detection tools like **Bubblemaps** (wallet clustering via transfer-graph analysis) and **RugCheck** (insider detection through network centrality), alongside real-time monitoring via **DexScreener** (bubblemaps.io, medium.com/@alchemist1411, wire.insiderfinance.io). Critical gaps exist in three areas: (1) **detection reliability**—Bubblemaps explicitly states its clustering is "heuristic, not ground truth" and "never legal proof" (web3.new/protocols/bubblemaps); (2) **enforcement mechanisms**—anti-sniping tools like Raydium CPI wrappers remain developer-initiated rather than platform-mandated (github.com/princedotrs/raydium-cpi); and (3) **regulatory specificity**—while MiCA and SEC enforcement target crypto manipulation broadly (esma.europa.eu, sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2026-34), the evidence lacks details on coordinated memecoin vamping schemes specifically. **Confidence: High** on tool capabilities and limitations; **Medium** on systematic adoption and regulatory application to vamping.

children.answer:
**Lessons from Previous Rug-Pull Waves:**

The 2017 ICO crash and 2021 BSC rug-pull wave ($2.8B lost per Chainalysis, CoinDesk) demonstrated that regulatory clarity and investor protection mechanisms are essential—the path from "2017's ICO chaos to today's regulated tokenisation" shows the industry learned "hard lessons about compliance" (investhub.org). High-profile scams like SaveTheKids (2021 pump-and-dump on BSC, Wikipedia) and NFT rug-pulls like Frosties and Evolved Apes (DappRadar, Halborn) spurred development of preventive tools: honeypot detectors, LP lock checkers, and smart contract scanners now analyze mint authority and ownership risks across multiple chains (OpenLiquid, CheckScan). The SEC's formation of a Cross-Border Task Force targeting pump-and-dump schemes (cryptonews.com) parallels traditional securities enforcement, signaling crypto market manipulation now faces similar regulatory scrutiny.

findings.result: LABSGuidesHow to Architect a DEX Against Front-Running and MEVA developer-focused guide on designing DEX smart contracts and systems to protect users from maximal extractable value (MEV) and predatory trading bots.introductionINTRODUCTIONHow to Architect a DEX Against Front-Running and MEVFront-running and Maximal Extractable Value (MEV) are critical threats to decentralized exchange (DEX) integrity. This guide explains the architectural principles and mechanisms to mitigate them.Front-running occurs when a malicious actor, typically a bot, observes a pending transaction in the mempool and submits their own transaction with a higher gas fee to execute first. This allows them to profit at the expense of the original user. MEV is a broader concept representing the total value that can be extracted from block production beyond standard block rewards, often through techniques like sandwich attacks, arbitrage, and liquidations. For DEXs, these activities lead to worse execution prices for users and can congest the network.The core vulnerability enabling MEV is transaction ordering. In permissionless blockchains like Ethereum, validators (or block builders) decide the sequence of transactions in a block. This creates a market where parties can pay to have their transactions placed in advantageous positions. To architect a resilient DEX, you must design systems that reduce the value of this privileged information or remove the ability to act on it. Key strategies include commit-reveal schemes, fair ordering protocols, and the use of private transaction relays.A foundational defense is implementing a commit-reveal scheme. In this two-phase process, a user first submits a cryptographic commitment (a hash) of their trade details. After a delay, they reveal the actual transaction. This prevents front-runners from knowing the trade's specifics during the vulnerable commitment phase. For example, a DEX like CowSwap uses batch auctions settled by a solver network, which aggregates orders and finds coincidence of wants (CoW) to minimize MEV exposure. This approach removes the transparent, single-transaction model that bots exploit.Another architectural approach is integrating with fair sequencing services or private mempools. Protocols like Flashbots Protect or Taichi Network allow users to submit transactions directly to block builders without exposing them to the public mempool. This denies general searchers the opportunity to front-run. When designing your DEX's transaction flow, you should provide users with an easy opt-in to these services, effectively creating a private order flow path that bypasses the most common attack surface.On-chain, careful smart contract design is crucial. Use mechanisms that obfuscate or finalize price impact instantly. Constant Function Market Makers (CFMMs) like Uniswap V2 are highly susceptible to sandwich attacks because price updates are public and sequential. Upgrading to concentrated liquidity (Uniswap V3) or implementing an instantaneous price oracle from a trusted provider can reduce the profitable window for MEV. Furthermore, consider threshold encryption for transactions until they are included in a block, as explored by Shutter Network.Ultimately, architecting against MEV requires a multi-layered strategy combining protocol-level changes, smart contract design, and user-facing tools. HomeDevelopersGuidesMEV Protection with Jito DontFrontTable of ContentsMEV Protection with Jito DontFrontMEV on SolanaWhat is a Sandwich Attack?How DontFront WorksStep by StepWhat Happens at the Block EngineBundle Ordering RulesAllowed PatternsMultiple DontFront Transactions in One BundleRejected PatternsIntegration ExamplesTypeScript (@solana/kit)Best PracticesTransaction-Level ProtectionDontFront-SpecificLimitationsReferencesEdit PageMEV Protection with Jito DontFront
Maximal Extractable Value (MEV) refers to the value that can be captured by
reordering, including, or excluding transactions within a block. MEV is not
unique to any one chain; it is a fundamental property of any blockchain where
block producers control transaction ordering. Some forms of MEV, like arbitrage,
help keep markets efficient by correcting price discrepancies across DEXs.
Others, like sandwich attacks, extract value directly from users. For developers
building DeFi applications, this can mean worse trade execution and lost
profits.
This guide focuses on sandwich attacks, a common form of harmful MEV, and how to
mitigate them using Jito's dontfront feature.
Recommended pre-reading:
Jito Bundles
MEV on Solana
Solana's architecture already reduces the MEV surface area compared to chains
with public mempools. Transactions are forwarded directly to the upcoming block
leader rather than sitting in a shared mempool, and unprocessed transactions
expire after roughly 150 blocks (~1 minute). This significantly narrows the
window for searchers to observe and act on pending transactions.
That said, MEV extraction still occurs. Searchers with optimized infrastructure
and direct validator connections can observe transaction flow and respond to it.
Much of the MEV activity on Solana is atomic arbitrage: bots that correct price
differences across DEXs in a single transaction. This type of MEV is generally
beneficial, as it improves price consistency across markets.
The harmful variety, sandwich attacks, is what developers should actively
protect against.
What is a Sandwich Attack?
A sandwich attack occurs when a searcher detects your pending swap and exploits
transaction ordering:

Front-run: the searcher buys the token before your trade, pushing the
price up
Your trade executes at a worse price than expected
Back-run: the searcher sells the token after your trade, pocketing the
difference

On Solana, this can happen through
Jito bundles. A
searcher submits a bundle of transactions like:
[frontrun_tx, your_tx, backrun_tx], and the block engine executes them
atomically in order. The victim gets a worse execution price while the searcher
profits from the spread.
How DontFront Works
DontFront is a
feature of the Jito block engine that prevents searchers from placing
transactions before yours in a bundle.
The mechanism: add any valid Solana public key that starts with jitodontfront
to any instruction in your transaction. The block engine recognizes this prefix
and enforces a rule: any bundle containing your transaction must place it at
index 0.
Without dontfront: [frontrun_tx, your_tx, backrun_tx] ← sandwich possible
With dontfront: [your_tx, ...] ← your tx must be first
Step by Step

Pick a valid address starting with jitodontfront (e.g.,
jitodontfront111111111111111111111111111111). The account does not need to
exist onchain; it is never read or written to. You can verify a string is a
valid address using the
assertIsAddress
function from @solana/kit or you can enter the addres

Computer Science > Cryptography and Security
 

 
 arXiv:2505.15383 (cs)
 

 
 
 [Submitted on 21 May 2025]
 Title:Real-Time Detection of Insider Threats Using Behavioral Analytics and Deep Evidential Clustering
 Authors:Anas Ali, Mubashar Husain, Peter Hans View a PDF of the paper titled Real-Time Detection of Insider Threats Using Behavioral Analytics and Deep Evidential Clustering, by Anas Ali and 2 other authors
 View PDF
 HTML (experimental)

 
 Abstract:Insider threats represent one of the most critical challenges in modern cybersecurity. These threats arise from individuals within an organization who misuse their legitimate access to harm the organization's assets, data, or operations. Traditional security mechanisms, primarily designed for external attackers, fall short in identifying these subtle and context-aware threats. In this paper, we propose a novel framework for real-time detection of insider threats using behavioral analytics combined with deep evidential clustering. Our system captures and analyzes user activities, applies context-rich behavioral features, and classifies potential threats using a deep evidential clustering model that estimates both cluster assignment and epistemic uncertainty. The proposed model dynamically adapts to behavioral changes and significantly reduces false positives. We evaluate our framework on benchmark insider threat datasets such as CERT and TWOS, achieving an average detection accuracy of 94.7% and a 38% reduction in false positives compared to traditional clustering methods. Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of integrating uncertainty modeling in threat detection pipelines. This research provides actionable insights for deploying intelligent, adaptive, and robust insider threat detection systems across various enterprise environments.

 Abstract
 This paper proposes and evaluates a novel real-time cybersecurity framework integrating artificial intelligence (AI) and blockchain technology to enhance the detection and auditability of cyber threats. Traditional cybersecurity approaches often lack transparency and robustness in logging and verifying AI-generated decisions, hindering forensic investigations and regulatory compliance. To address these challenges, we developed an integrated solution combining a convolutional neural network (CNN)-based anomaly detection module with a permissioned Ethereum blockchain to securely log and immutably store AI-generated alerts and relevant metadata. The proposed system employs smart contracts to automatically validate AI alerts and ensure data integrity and transparency, significantly enhancing auditability and forensic analysis capabilities. To rigorously test and validate our solution, we conducted comprehensive experiments using the CICIDS2017 dataset and evaluated the system’s detection accuracy, precision, recall, and real-time responsiveness. Additionally, we performed penetration testing and security assessments to verify system resilience against common cybersecurity threats. Results demonstrate that our AI-blockchain integrated solution achieves superior detection performance while ensuring real-time logging, transparency, and auditability. The integration significantly strengthens system robustness, reduces false positives, and provides clear benefits for cybersecurity management, especially in regulated environments. This paper concludes by outlining potential avenues for future research, particularly extending blockchain scalability, privacy enhancements, and optimizing performance for high-throughput cybersecurity applications.
 
 
 Keywords: AI-based threat detection; blockchain; smart contract; cyber security; metadata integrity; real-time system auditability; real-time system auditability; anomaly detection
